sections.paragraph:
( Deu 28:42 [in pause צְלָצָל]), constr. צִלְצַל ( Job 40:31 , and Isa 18:1), pl. צֶלְצְלִים, see No. 2 (from the root צָלַל No. I.)-

(1) a tingling, clinking (compare as to these geminate forms, Hupfeld, Exercitt. Eth. p. 28; my Hebr. Gram. p. 119; Arab. صَاْصَلَ to tinkle, to clink, as a bridle or bell), used of a tinkling of metal when struck, of arms, the sound of wings (see No. 3). Hence, Isa 18:1, אֶרֶץ צִלְצַל כְּנָפַיִם “the land of the clangour of armies,” i.e. full of hosts, striking together their arms with clangour, Land voll (waffen=) klirrender (Heeres=) flügel, i.e. Ethiopia. Armies in this passage are called wings (see כָּנָף No. 1 ), and in this double use of the word there is an ingenious play of words, since צלצל is also used of the sound of flapping of wings (vom Schwirren). The opinions of others are discussed in my Comment. on this passage.

(2) a tinkling instrument, specially a fish spear, Job 40:31 . Pl. צֶלְצְלִים, constr. state צִלְצְלֵי cymbals (die Becken), not unlike those now used for military purposes, 2Sa 6:5 Psa 150:5.

(3) a stridulous insect, a cricket, Deu 28:42.
